story_title: Israel–Lebanon buffer zone and ongoing strikes
tldr: Headlines divide across five frames: Israeli military strikes and civilian casualties (dominant, 5 buckets), ceasefire and withdrawal negotiations, attacks on medics as moral violation, Hezbollah resistance framing, and a lived-experience human-interest angle. Iran_state is the most isolated bucket (0.485), framing the story entirely through Hezbollah resistance and Iranian support rather than Israeli operations.
event_summary: Israel has continued airstrikes in southern Lebanon, killing at least 22 people according to Lebanon's health ministry; negotiations over an IDF withdrawal contingent on Hezbollah disarmament are ongoing; MSF has documented an alarming pattern of attacks on medical personnel.

evidence.quote: Israeli strikes in southern Lebanon kill 22 people, health ministry says
Israel strikes cars on Lebanese highway, killing 8, including 2 children
Lebanon seeks truce extension as Israeli strikes continue
In Lebanon, the idea of peace with Israel continues to deeply divide
Israeli attacks on medics in southern Lebanon part of 'alarming pattern,' says MSF
Hezbollah chief hails Iran support for resistance, vows not to surrender to Israel
Sport helping displaced Lebanese living in stadium to cope
Inside the Christian border town in Lebanon that says it survived by defying Hezbollah

isolation_top.note: IRNA headline frames the story through Hezbollah resistance and Iranian solidarity — the only bucket in which the dominant voice is the non-state armed actor rather than Israeli military action or Lebanese civilian casualties.

US headline (Christian border town that 'survived by defying Hezbollah') foregrounds sectarian community resilience — a cultural-identity and lived-experience frame entirely absent from the conflict-and-diplomacy coverage dominant elsewhere.
France 24 foregrounds Lebanese societal division over peace with Israel — a domestic public-opinion framing rather than the military-operations or humanitarian frames dominant in other buckets.

DW leads with displaced Lebanese using sport to cope in stadium shelters — a humanitarian resilience angle that diverges from the military-casualty or negotiation framing all other outlets prioritize.

CBC uniquely foregrounds the pattern of attacks on medical personnel (MSF attribution) — a morality/IHL frame absent from the five SECURITY_DEFENSE buckets that cover the same strikes through a casualty-count lens.

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only outlet in the corpus to headline attacks on medics explicitly as an 'alarming pattern' documented by MSF — a humanitarian law and morality frame absent from all other buckets covering the same strikes.

The only headline that foregrounds the non-state actor perspective — Hezbollah's vow not to surrender and Iran's support — diverging from all other outlets which frame the story through Israeli military action or Lebanese civilian impact.

Frames the story through internal Lebanese societal fracture over the prospect of peace with Israel — the only headline in the corpus that centers Lebanese public opinion rather than Israeli operations or Hezbollah resistance.

bottom_line: The small corpus (14 articles, 10 buckets) produces five distinct headline frames with no single dominant cluster: SECURITY_DEFENSE (strikes and casualties) covers the most buckets but barely half. The most analytically significant divergences are IRNA's resistance-solidarity framing and Canada's medical-personnel morality frame — both treat the same Israeli military operations through entirely different ethical lenses. Germany and the USA produce the only human-interest and cultural-identity angles, diverging from the conflict register that unifies the remaining eight outlets.
